content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 16, 2002

Ms. Marcie Burgess, Administrative Assistant
Anaheim Police Officers PAC
508 No. Anaheim Boulevard
Anaheim, CA 92805-2647

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-250

Dear Ms. Burgess:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Anaheim
Police Officers Political Action Committee regarding the provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Given that the Anaheim Police Officers Political Action Committee ("APO
PAC") plans to make independent expenditures in the form of mailings for the candidates
that the PAC has endorsed, may board members of the Anaheim Police Officers Political
Action Committee, or regular members, walk precincts for candidates that the committee
has endorsed using the candidate's materials and walk lists?

CONCLUSION

Volunteer precinct walking by either the board members or regular members of
APO PAC does not, by itself, constitute "coordination" with a candidate which would
render independent expenditures made by APO PAC on behalf of endorsed candidates
ineligible contributions. However, you should caution APO PAC members who wish to walk
precincts for an endorsed candidate not to have any discussions about the PAC's plans for
independent expenditures with the candidate or his or her campaign staff.

FACTS

APO PAC is gearing up for Anaheim's local mayoral and city council elections.
APO PAC plans to make independent expenditures in the form of mailings for the

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-250
Page No. 2

candidates that the PAC has endorsed. You have received conflicting advice from an
independent source about whether APO PAC's board members and/or its regular
members can walk precincts for candidates endorsed by the PAC using walk lists and
hand-out materials provided by the candidates. You were advised that the board
members could not do such precinct walking, but that the regular members could.

In addition, sometimes the firefighters in Anaheim undertake their own precinct
walking efforts on behalf of candidates. Since the Anaheim Police Officers PAC has
endorsed most of the same candidates as the firefighters, you also ask whether APO PAC
members may walk precincts with the firefighters for endorsed candidates, using walk
pieces provided by the firefighters.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines an "independent expenditure" in section 82031 as follows:

"'Independent expenditure' means an expenditure made by any person
in connection with a communication which expressly advocates the
election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate or the qualification,
passage or defeat of a clearly identified measure, or taken as a whole and
in context, unambiguously urges a particular result in an election but
which is not made to or at the behest of the affected candidate or
committee."²

Section 85500(b), added by Proposition 34, provides additional clarification as to
when expenditures will or will not be considered independent under the Act:

"(b) An expenditure may not be considered independent, and shall be
treated as a contribution from the person making the expenditure to the
candidate on whose behalf, or for whose benefit, the expenditure is made
under any of the following circumstances:
(1) The expenditure is made with the cooperation of, or in consultation
with, the candidate on whose behalf, or for whose benefit, the expenditure
is made, or any controlled committee or any agent of the candidate.
(2) The expenditure is made in concert with, or at the request or
suggestion of, the candidate on whose behalf, or for whose benefit, the
expenditure is made, or any controlled committee or any agent of the
candidate.
(3) The expenditure is made under any arrangement, coordination, or
direction with respect to the candidate or the candidate's agent and the
person making the expenditure."

_______________________

² The case Governor Gray Davis Com. v. American Taxpayers Alliance, __ Cal.Rptr.2d __, 2002 WL
31116829, 2 Cal. Daily Op. Serv. 9916 (Cal.App. 1 Dist., Sep 25, 2002) (No. A096658) was recently
decided which interprets express advocacy. Your question, however, does not raise express advocacy
issues.

File No. A-02-250
Page No. 3

As you were advised by the FPPC's Technical Assistance Division by phone, the
regulation concerning independent expenditures is being revised and will be considered
at the Commission's December meeting. The rulemaking proposes to extensively revise
current regulation 18225.7, to more clearly and specifically define conduct that
constitutes coordination with a candidate and thereby renders expenditures contributions
to the candidate under the Act, rather than independent expenditures. You can find the
draft regulation and information about the rulemaking on the Commission's website at
www.fppc.ca.gov under the "Commission Agendas" section. When the regulation is
adopted it will provide you with more specific guidance as to what conduct constitutes
"coordination" with a candidate. Considering that the November 5, 2002, election is
approaching, however, and the new regulation will not be adopted before the election, we
can provide you with guidance concerning independent expenditures under the statute
and the current regulation.

Precinct walking by either board members or regular members of APO PAC using
walk pieces provided by the campaign does not, by itself, constitute "coordination" with
a candidate which would render independent expenditures made by APO PAC on behalf
of endorsed candidates contributions. For the PAC's expenditures to be independent and
not count as a contribution to a candidate, the expenditures must be made by the PAC
without any cooperation or consultation with the candidate who benefits from the
expenditure, or that candidate's controlled committee or agent. (Section 85500(b).) The
expenditure may not be made in concert with or at the request or suggestion of the
candidate. (Section 85500(b).)

Therefore, you should caution APO PAC members who wish to walk precincts
for an endorsed candidate not to have any discussions about the PAC's plans for
independent expenditures with the candidate or the candidate's campaign staff. Certainly
the risk that PAC expenditures for mailers on behalf of a candidate will be discussed with
the candidate is heightened if board members of the PAC undertake volunteer activities
in the candidate's campaign. And if a PAC board member held a formal policy making
position with the candidate's campaign or provided advice regarding campaign strategy
or fundraising, it would call into question the independence of the PAC's expenditures on
behalf of the candidate. However, if a board member or a regular member undertakes
ministerial volunteer activities on behalf of a campaign such as precinct walking or
envelope stuffing, that activity, in and of itself, would not impair the independence of the
PAC's expenditures.

With respect to your question about whether APO PAC members may walk
precincts with the firefighters for endorsed candidates, a similar analysis applies. The
independence of PAC expenditures will not be affected if APO PAC members walk
precincts with firefighters for candidates endorsed by APO PAC. However, APO PAC
members may not discuss APO PAC expenditures on behalf of a candidate with that
candidate or with any campaign staff or agents of that candidate's campaign.

File No. A-02-250
Page No. 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
